questions.question: The Indian Parliament consists of how many Houses?
questions.explanation: Indian Parliament has two Houses: Lok Sabha (Lower House) and Rajya Sabha (Upper House), plus the President.

questions.question: What is the maximum strength of Lok Sabha?
questions.explanation: The maximum strength of Lok Sabha is 552: up to 530 from States, up to 20 from UTs, and 2 nominated Anglo-Indians (though the 2 nominated seats were abolished by 104th Amendment).

questions.question: Who is the ex-officio Chairman of the Rajya Sabha?
questions.explanation: The Vice President of India is the ex-officio Chairman of the Rajya Sabha.

questions.question: The Rajya Sabha is also called the:
questions.explanation: Rajya Sabha is officially called the 'Council of States'. Lok Sabha is called the 'House of the People'.

questions.question: What is the term of a Rajya Sabha member?
questions.explanation: Rajya Sabha members serve 6-year terms. One-third retire every 2 years. Rajya Sabha is a permanent body.

questions.question: The President of India is elected by:
options: Elected members of Parliament and State Legislatures
questions.explanation: The President is elected by an Electoral College consisting of elected members of both Houses of Parliament and elected members of all State Legislative Assemblies.

questions.question: Which article empowers the Parliament to create new States or alter the boundaries of existing States?
questions.explanation: Article 3 empowers Parliament to form new States by separation, union, or merger of existing States, or by altering boundaries, names, or areas.

questions.question: What is the quorum required to conduct business in Lok Sabha?
questions.explanation: The quorum to constitute a meeting of either House is 1/10th of total membership.

questions.question: How many members can the President nominate to the Rajya Sabha?
questions.explanation: The President nominates 12 members to Rajya Sabha who have distinguished themselves in literature, science, art, or social service.

questions.question: Which article of the Indian Constitution deals with the formation of new States?
questions.explanation: Article 3 empowers Parliament to admit new States, form new States from existing ones, alter boundaries or change names.

questions.question: What is the minimum age for a person to become a member of Rajya Sabha?
questions.explanation: A person must be at least 30 years old to be elected to the Rajya Sabha.

questions.question: How is the Speaker of Lok Sabha elected?
options: Elected by all Lok Sabha members
questions.explanation: The Speaker of Lok Sabha is elected from among the members of the Lok Sabha by its members.

questions.question: The Indian Parliament consists of:
options: President, Lok Sabha, and Rajya Sabha
Lok Sabha, Rajya Sabha, and Supreme Court
questions.explanation: The Indian Parliament consists of the President + Lok Sabha (House of the People) + Rajya Sabha (Council of States). Article 79. The President is an integral part of Parliament (though not a member of either House).

questions.question: The maximum strength of the Lok Sabha is:
options: 552 (530 elected from states + 20 from UTs + 2 Anglo-Indians, but Anglo-Indian nomination abolished in 2020)
questions.explanation: Maximum strength: 552 (Article 81): 530 members from states + 20 from Union Territories + (formerly 2 Anglo-Indian nominated members, abolished by 104th Amendment, 2020). Current effective strength: 543 elected seats.

questions.question: The term of the Lok Sabha is:
options: 5 years (can be extended during National Emergency)
questions.explanation: The normal term of the Lok Sabha is 5 years from the date of its first sitting after a general election (Article 83). During a National Emergency (Article 352), Parliament can extend its term by 1 year at a time by law.

questions.question: The Rajya Sabha is a permanent body because:
options: It is never dissolved — only 1/3 of its members retire every 2 years
It has more powers than Lok Sabha
Its members are elected for 10 years
questions.explanation: Rajya Sabha is a permanent body (Article 83) — it is never dissolved. Each member serves a 6-year term, and 1/3 of its members retire every 2 years. Currently has 245 members (238 elected + 12 nominated by President for expertise in arts, science, literature, social service).

questions.question: A Money Bill can only be introduced in:
questions.explanation: A Money Bill (Article 110) can only be introduced in the Lok Sabha. After Lok Sabha passes it, it is sent to Rajya Sabha which can only make recommendations (not amend/reject). If no action in 14 days, it is deemed passed. Lok Sabha has final say on Money Bills.

questions.question: The Speaker of the Lok Sabha is elected by:
options: Members of Lok Sabha from among themselves
questions.explanation: The Speaker of the Lok Sabha is elected by the members of the Lok Sabha from among themselves (Article 93). The Speaker maintains order, presides over proceedings, and is the guardian of the powers and privileges of the House.

questions.question: A 'No Confidence Motion' can be moved against:
options: The Council of Ministers (the Cabinet) in the Lok Sabha
questions.explanation: A No Confidence Motion (or Motion of No Confidence) is moved in the Lok Sabha against the Council of Ministers. If passed, the Prime Minister and Cabinet must resign. Requires support of at least 50 Lok Sabha members to be admitted.

questions.question: The Vice-President of India is elected by:
options: Members of Electoral College (same as President)
Members of both Houses of Parliament (Lok Sabha + Rajya Sabha) — not state legislatures
questions.explanation: The Vice-President is elected by members of both Houses of Parliament (Article 66) — Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha. State legislators are NOT part of this electoral process (unlike President's election).

questions.question: The Prime Minister is appointed by:
options: President of India — who appoints the leader of the majority party/coalition in Lok Sabha
questions.explanation: The President appoints the Prime Minister (Article 75). Conventionally, the President appoints the leader of the party/coalition that commands majority in the Lok Sabha. The PM is the head of the Council of Ministers and real executive head.

questions.question: President's Rule (State Emergency) is imposed under:
options: Article 356 (if state government cannot function according to Constitution — on Governor's report or otherwise)
questions.explanation: Article 356 (President's Rule/State Emergency): The President can take over state government if state constitutional machinery fails. Proclaimed on Governor's report (or other information). Parliament must approve every 6 months. Maximum: 3 years.

questions.question: A judge of the Supreme Court can be removed by:
options: President on the recommendation of PM
An address of Parliament (both Houses) by special majority presented to the President (impeachment)
questions.explanation: A Supreme Court judge can only be removed by the President on an address of Parliament — both Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha must pass the resolution by a special majority (majority of total membership + 2/3 of members present and voting). This is called impeachment.

questions.question: The Seventh Schedule of the Constitution contains:
options: Three lists — Union List (97 subjects), State List (66 subjects), Concurrent List (47 subjects)
questions.explanation: The Seventh Schedule (Article 246) contains three legislative lists: Union List (97 subjects on which only Parliament can legislate), State List (66 subjects for states), and Concurrent List (47 subjects where both can legislate — in conflict, Union law prevails).

questions.question: The Parliamentary form of government in India is borrowed from:
questions.explanation: Parliamentary system (Cabinet government, Prime Minister's role, collective responsibility) is borrowed from the British (Westminster) model. Also known as Westminster Model.

questions.question: The maximum gap between two sessions of Parliament is:
questions.explanation: The Constitution requires that Parliament must meet at least twice a year and the gap between two sessions must not exceed 6 months (Article 85).

questions.question: Money Bills can be introduced only in:
questions.explanation: Money Bills (Article 110) can be introduced only in Lok Sabha (Article 109). Rajya Sabha can only delay it for 14 days but cannot amend or reject it.

questions.question: The Rajya Sabha is a:
questions.explanation: Rajya Sabha is a permanent body — it is never dissolved as a whole. One-third of its members retire every two years (6-year term for each member).

questions.question: Maximum membership of Lok Sabha:
questions.explanation: Lok Sabha maximum strength: 552 (530 from states + 20 from UTs + 2 Anglo-Indians nominated by President). Currently operational strength is 543 (2 Anglo-Indian nominated seats abolished by 104th Amendment).

questions.question: The Deputy Speaker of Lok Sabha is elected by:
questions.explanation: The Deputy Speaker of Lok Sabha is elected by the members of Lok Sabha from among themselves (Article 93). Similarly, the Deputy Chairman of Rajya Sabha is elected by its members.

questions.question: Zero Hour in Parliament refers to:
questions.explanation: Zero Hour (informal institution) begins at noon (12:00) after Question Hour, allowing members to raise urgent public matters without prior notice to the Speaker.

questions.question: A joint sitting of Parliament is presided over by:
questions.explanation: Joint sitting of both Houses (Article 108) in case of deadlock is presided over by the Speaker of Lok Sabha (or Deputy Speaker in Speaker's absence).

questions.question: The Budget is presented by the:
questions.explanation: The Union Budget (Annual Financial Statement) is presented by the Finance Minister in the Lok Sabha (traditionally on the last working day of February, now changed to February 1).

questions.question: Quorum in the Lok Sabha is:
questions.explanation: Quorum for Lok Sabha (Article 100): 1/10th of total membership = 55 members (out of 543). Similarly for Rajya Sabha: 1/10th = 25 members (out of 250).

questions.question: Which type of majority is required to pass an Ordinary Bill?
options: Simple majority (majority of members present and voting)
questions.explanation: Ordinary Bills require simple majority — majority of members present and voting (not absolute majority of total membership). Constitutional Amendments require special majority.

questions.question: Minimum age to become President of India:
questions.explanation: To be eligible for President: Must be a citizen of India, minimum 35 years of age, qualified to be a member of Lok Sabha (Article 58).

questions.question: Which article gives Parliament the power to create new states?
questions.explanation: Article 3 empowers Parliament to form new states, increase/decrease areas, alter boundaries or change names of existing states. Such bills require Presidential recommendation.

questions.question: The minimum number of members required to sign a notice of No-Confidence Motion is:
questions.explanation: A No-Confidence Motion can be introduced in Lok Sabha if at least 50 members support it (notice signed by minimum 50 members). The Speaker then admits the motion.

questions.question: Parliamentary privileges are codified in:
questions.explanation: Parliamentary privileges are mentioned in Articles 105 (Parliament) and 194 (State Legislatures). They are NOT fully codified — courts have ruled that privileges exist as they did in British Parliament in 1947.

questions.question: The Speaker of Lok Sabha vacates office:
options: When new Lok Sabha meets for the first time
questions.explanation: The Speaker vacates office not when Lok Sabha is dissolved, but 'immediately before the first sitting of the new Lok Sabha' (Article 94). The Speaker thus continues for the first sitting of the new House.

questions.question: Under which article can Parliament legislate on State List subjects?
questions.explanation: Parliament can legislate on State List under: Art. 249 (RS resolution for national interest), Art. 250 (during Emergency), Art. 252 (2 or more states request), Art. 253 (for treaty/agreement).

questions.question: The maximum size of the Council of Ministers cannot exceed:
questions.explanation: 91st Constitutional Amendment (2003): Council of Ministers strength shall not exceed 15% of total membership of the Lower House (Lok Sabha). Similarly for state cabinets.

questions.question: Rajya Sabha's special power to authorize Parliament to legislate on State subjects is under:
questions.explanation: Article 249: If Rajya Sabha passes a resolution by 2/3rd majority that it is necessary in national interest, Parliament can legislate on a State List subject for 1 year (renewable).

questions.question: Rajya Sabha can authorize creation of new All India Services under:
questions.explanation: Article 312: Rajya Sabha can authorize Parliament to create new All India Services (like IAS, IPS, IFS) by 2/3rd majority resolution, if it declares such services as necessary in national interest.

questions.question: A Constitutional Amendment Bill rejected by Rajya Sabha:
options: Cannot be passed through joint sitting
questions.explanation: Constitutional Amendment Bills CANNOT be passed through a joint sitting of Parliament (unlike ordinary bills which can). Rajya Sabha's rejection of a Constitutional Amendment Bill means it fails.

questions.question: A political party gets National Party status if:
options: Wins 2% of Lok Sabha seats from 3 states OR recognized in 4 states
questions.explanation: A party is recognized as a National Party if: (a) Secures at least 6% of votes in 4 states and 4 Lok Sabha seats, OR (b) Wins at least 2% of total Lok Sabha seats from at least 3 different states.

questions.question: The Delimitation Commission is established by:
questions.explanation: Delimitation Commission is established by an Act of Parliament (Delimitation Act). It determines boundaries of constituencies for Lok Sabha and State Legislative Assemblies.

questions.question: 'First Past the Post' electoral system used in India means:
options: Candidate with most votes wins even if less than 50%
questions.explanation: First Past the Post (FPTP) or Simple Plurality System: The candidate with the highest number of votes wins, even if they have less than 50% of votes. Used for Lok Sabha and Vidhan Sabha elections.

questions.question: Election to Rajya Sabha is conducted through:
options: Proportional representation by Single Transferable Vote
questions.explanation: Rajya Sabha members are elected by elected members of State Legislative Assemblies through Proportional Representation by Single Transferable Vote (PR-STV).

questions.question: VVPATs (Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trails) were introduced in India in:
questions.explanation: VVPATs were first used in India in the Nagaland state election in 2013. They were used in all Lok Sabha constituencies from 2019 elections.

questions.question: Article 370 of the Constitution dealt with:
questions.explanation: Article 370 (now abrogated by Presidential Order in August 2019 ratified by Parliament) gave special autonomous status to J&K, limiting Parliament's legislative powers over J&K.

questions.question: 'Privilege Motion' in Parliament is moved against:
options: Ministers/members for breach of parliamentary privilege
questions.explanation: Privilege Motion is moved when a member believes that a minister or another member has committed a breach of parliamentary privilege (e.g., misleading the House, contempt of Parliament).

questions.question: Which feature was borrowed from the Australian Constitution?
options: Concurrent List and joint sitting of Parliament
questions.explanation: From Australia: Concurrent List (Concurrent Legislative List), joint sitting of Parliament (Art 108), and the term 'Commonwealth'. The Parliamentary system came from Britain.

questions.question: The concept of 'Rule of Law' in the Indian Constitution was borrowed from:
questions.explanation: Rule of Law, Parliamentary system, Cabinet government, bicameral Parliament, and the Speaker's role were all borrowed from the British Constitution (Westminster model).

questions.question: A Money Bill can be introduced in:
questions.explanation: A Money Bill (as defined under Art 110) can be introduced only in the Lok Sabha on the recommendation of the President. Rajya Sabha can only recommend amendments — Lok Sabha may or may not accept.

questions.question: The Rajya Sabha cannot be dissolved because:
questions.explanation: Rajya Sabha is a permanent body — it cannot be dissolved. One-third of its members retire every two years. Its members are elected for 6-year terms by elected members of State Legislative Assemblies.

questions.question: The presiding officer of a Joint Sitting of Parliament is:
questions.explanation: Joint sittings (Art 108) are presided over by the Speaker of Lok Sabha, or in his absence, the Deputy Speaker of Lok Sabha, or if he is also absent, the Deputy Chairman of Rajya Sabha.

questions.question: A joint sitting of Parliament CANNOT be held for:
options: An ordinary bill deadlocked between Houses
A bill passed by Lok Sabha but rejected by Rajya Sabha
A bill on which Rajya Sabha amends excessively
questions.explanation: Joint sittings cannot be held for Constitution Amendment Bills (need special majority in each House separately), Money Bills, or Financial Bills. Only for ordinary bills under Art 108.

questions.question: Who can summon and prorogue sessions of Parliament?
options: President on the advice of Cabinet
questions.explanation: The President summons each House of Parliament (Art 85), prorogues the Houses, and dissolves the Lok Sabha — always on the advice of the Council of Ministers (Cabinet).

questions.question: The maximum gap allowed between two sessions of Parliament is:
questions.explanation: Article 85(1) requires that the gap between two sessions of Parliament shall not exceed 6 months. This means Parliament must meet at least twice a year (Budget session and Monsoon/Winter session).

questions.question: Which Parliamentary Committee is called the 'watchdog of public finance'?
questions.explanation: The Public Accounts Committee (PAC) examines the appropriation accounts and audit reports of the CAG. It verifies whether money was spent for the purpose it was appropriated. PAC has 22 members.

questions.question: The Estimates Committee of Parliament has how many members?
questions.explanation: The Estimates Committee has 30 members, all from Lok Sabha. It examines the estimates in the budget and suggests economies in expenditure. Rajya Sabha has no representation.

questions.question: The term 'prorogation' of Parliament means:
options: Termination of a session without dissolution
questions.explanation: Prorogation terminates a session of Parliament. Bills pending at prorogation lapse (except those before joint committees or passed by one House). Adjournment only terminates a sitting for a shorter period.

questions.question: Which Article deals with the impeachment of the President?
questions.explanation: Article 61 deals with impeachment of the President. It can be initiated in either House of Parliament for 'violation of the Constitution'. It requires a 2/3rd majority in both Houses separately.

questions.question: The President's 'Pocket Veto' refers to:
options: Returning a bill for reconsideration indefinitely without acting
Sending to Supreme Court for opinion
questions.explanation: Pocket Veto is an informal term for the President's power to keep a bill pending without returning it — effectively killing it. This has been used once by President Zail Singh (1986, Postal Amendment Bill).

questions.question: The Vice President of India can be removed from office by:
options: A resolution of Rajya Sabha agreed to by Lok Sabha
questions.explanation: The Vice President can be removed by a resolution of the Rajya Sabha (passed by a majority of its members) agreed to by the Lok Sabha. No specific grounds are needed, unlike the President's impeachment.

questions.question: The 91st Constitutional Amendment (2003) limits the size of the Council of Ministers to:
options: 15% of the total strength of the Lower House
questions.explanation: The 91st Amendment (2003) inserted Art 75(1A) limiting the Council of Ministers to not more than 15% of the total membership of Lok Sabha. Same limit applies to state Cabinets (Art 164(1A)).

questions.question: Which Article requires ministers to be members of Parliament within 6 months?
questions.explanation: Article 75(5) states a minister who is not a member of either House of Parliament for 6 consecutive months shall cease to be a minister.

questions.question: The concept of 'Judicial Review' in India is:
options: Limited — courts can review laws for constitutional validity but not policy
questions.explanation: India has a limited form of judicial review — courts can strike down laws violating the Constitution (unlike USA's stronger judicial review). The Basic Structure doctrine further limits Parliament's amendment power.

questions.question: During a National Emergency, the term of Lok Sabha can be extended by Parliament for:
questions.explanation: During a National Emergency, Parliament can extend the term of Lok Sabha by one year at a time for any length of time, but not beyond 6 months after the Emergency ceases.

questions.question: The maximum strength of Lok Sabha as provided in the Constitution is:
questions.explanation: Article 81 provides that Lok Sabha shall not consist of more than 552 members — 530 from States, 20 from UTs, and 2 nominated Anglo-Indians. Currently 543 are elected; Anglo-Indian nomination was removed by 104th Amendment.

questions.question: A member of Lok Sabha can be disqualified if he/she is absent for how long without permission?
questions.explanation: Article 101(4) states a member of either House of Parliament shall be disqualified if he/she is absent from all meetings of the House for a period of 60 days without permission.

questions.question: The Speaker of Lok Sabha is elected by:
questions.explanation: The Speaker of Lok Sabha is elected by the members of Lok Sabha from among themselves (Art 93). The Speaker can be removed by a resolution of the House by a majority of all the then members.

questions.question: A bill is a 'Money Bill' if it deals with:
options: Imposition of taxes and appropriation from Consolidated Fund
questions.explanation: Article 110 defines a Money Bill as dealing with imposition, abolition, remission, alteration, regulation of taxes; regulation of public debt; custody of Consolidated Fund; appropriation from Consolidated Fund, etc.

questions.question: The Budget is presented in Parliament by:
questions.explanation: The Union Budget (Annual Financial Statement under Art 112) is presented by the Finance Minister in Lok Sabha. The President causes it to be laid before both Houses.

questions.question: 'Cut Motions' in Parliament can be moved only on:
questions.explanation: Cut Motions are moved in Lok Sabha on Demands for Grants as part of the Budget process. There are 3 types: Policy Cut, Economy Cut, and Token Cut. They express disapproval of government policy.

questions.question: The Rajya Sabha has special powers to resolve that Parliament can legislate on State subjects under:
questions.explanation: Art 249: Rajya Sabha by 2/3rd majority can declare it necessary for Parliament to legislate on a State subject in national interest. Art 250: During National Emergency, Parliament can legislate on State List.

questions.question: The Governor's address to the State Legislature at the commencement of session corresponds to:
options: President's address to Parliament at the commencement of first session of the year
questions.explanation: Just as the President addresses both Houses of Parliament at the commencement of the first session after each election and the first session of each year (Art 87), the Governor addresses the State Legislature (Art 176).

questions.question: VVPAT (Voter Verified Paper Audit Trail) was introduced in which election?
questions.explanation: VVPATs were first used in the 2013 Nagaland Assembly elections as a pilot. The Supreme Court ordered 5 VVPAT slips per constituency to be counted in the 2019 Lok Sabha elections.

questions.question: The Delimitation Commission is established under:
questions.explanation: The Delimitation Commission is constituted by the President under the Delimitation Commission Act. It redraws Lok Sabha and State Assembly constituencies based on Census data. After 2002, constituencies were frozen till 2026.

questions.question: The concept of 'Separation of Powers' in India is:
options: Not rigidly followed — there is functional overlap but no concentration
questions.explanation: India does not follow strict separation of powers like USA. There is functional overlap — Parliament is both legislative and can exercise some judicial functions (contempt); President is executive but also part of Parliament.

questions.question: Which Schedule contains the Oaths and Affirmations for constitutional offices?
questions.explanation: The 3rd Schedule contains forms of Oaths or Affirmations for various constitutional offices — Union Ministers, candidates for Parliament, Members of Parliament, President, Judges of Supreme Court and High Courts.

questions.question: The 2nd Schedule of the Constitution deals with:
options: Salaries and allowances of constitutional office holders
Allocation of seats in Rajya Sabha
questions.explanation: The 2nd Schedule (Provisions as to emoluments, allowances, privileges, etc.) deals with salaries of the President, Governors, Judges of Supreme Court and High Courts, CAG, Speaker, and Deputy Speaker.

questions.question: The 4th Schedule of the Constitution relates to:
options: Allocation of seats in the Rajya Sabha
questions.explanation: The 4th Schedule contains the allocation of seats in the Rajya Sabha to States and Union Territories. UP has the highest number (31), followed by Maharashtra (19) and Tamil Nadu (18).

questions.question: Which Article enables Parliament to form new All India Services?
questions.explanation: Article 312 empowers Rajya Sabha (by 2/3rd resolution) to declare creation of new All India Services in national interest. Under this, IAS, IPS and Indian Forest Service (IFS) were created. Parliament then makes law.

questions.question: Who has the power to create or abolish a State Legislative Council?
options: Parliament on request of the State Legislature
questions.explanation: Parliament can create or abolish a State Legislative Council under Art 169 — but only if the State Legislative Assembly passes a resolution for this by special majority. Parliament then makes law (simple majority).

questions.question: What is the quorum required to conduct business in the Lok Sabha?
questions.explanation: The quorum to constitute a meeting of either House of Parliament is one-tenth of the total number of members of the House (Art 100). For Lok Sabha (543 members), this means at least 55 members.

questions.question: The Comptroller and Auditor General of India is appointed by:
questions.explanation: The CAG is appointed by the President of India (Art 148). The CAG can be removed only by the same process as a Supreme Court judge — by an address of both Houses of Parliament (special majority).

questions.question: Rajya Sabha members who are nominated by the President are experts in:
options: Literature, science, art, and social service
questions.explanation: Article 80(3) says the President nominates 12 members to Rajya Sabha who have special knowledge or practical experience in literature, science, art and social service — to enrich the quality of debate.

questions.question: Which is the correct order of precedence: who comes after the Vice President?
questions.explanation: India's official order of precedence: 1. President, 2. Vice President, 3. Prime Minister, 4. Governors (in their states), 5. Former Presidents, 6. Deputy PM, 7. Chief Justice of India / Speaker of Lok Sabha (equal).

questions.question: The maximum strength of Rajya Sabha is:
questions.explanation: Article 80 states the Council of States (Rajya Sabha) shall consist of not more than 250 members — 238 elected from States and UTs, plus 12 nominated by the President. Currently 245 members (233 elected + 12 nominated).

questions.question: Under which Article can the Supreme Court review its own judgments?
questions.explanation: Article 137 empowers the Supreme Court to review any judgment pronounced or order made by it, subject to any law made by Parliament or rules made under Art 145. Review petitions must be filed within 30 days.

questions.question: The Union List contains subjects on which:
options: Both Parliament and States can legislate
Neither Parliament nor States can legislate
questions.explanation: The Union List (97 items) contains subjects on which only the Parliament has exclusive legislative power.

questions.question: What is the maximum gap allowed between two sessions of Parliament?
questions.explanation: The Constitution requires that no more than 6 months should elapse between the last sitting of one session and the first sitting of the next session.

questions.question: The 'Triple Test' or Kesavananda Bharati case (1973) established which doctrine?
questions.explanation: Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala (1973) established that Parliament cannot amend the 'basic structure' of the Constitution.

questions.question: The Concurrent List contains subjects on which:
options: Both Centre and States can legislate
questions.explanation: The Concurrent List (52 items) contains subjects on which both the Parliament and State Legislatures can make laws. In case of conflict, Central law prevails.

questions.question: Under Article 110, a Bill is deemed to be a Money Bill if it deals with:
options: Imposition, abolition, remission, alteration or regulation of any tax
Appropriation of money from Consolidated Fund
questions.explanation: Article 110 defines a Money Bill. It deals with: taxation, borrowing, Consolidated Fund, expenditure charged on Consolidated Fund, appropriation, etc.

questions.question: The 'First Past The Post' (FPTP) electoral system used in Lok Sabha elections means:
options: Candidate with most votes wins, even without majority
Second round run-off if no majority
questions.explanation: In FPTP, the candidate who gets the most votes (plurality) in a constituency wins, even if they do not have an absolute majority.

questions.question: Which article provides immunity to members of Parliament from legal proceedings for anything said in Parliament?
questions.explanation: Article 105(2) provides that no member of Parliament shall be liable to any proceedings in any court in respect of anything said or any vote given by him in Parliament.

questions.question: Which article of the Constitution enables the Governor to reserve a Bill for the President's consideration?
questions.explanation: Article 200 provides that the Governor may, instead of giving assent to a State Bill, reserve it for the consideration of the President.

questions.question: Under which constitutional provision does Parliament have residuary powers (power to legislate on subjects not in any list)?
questions.explanation: Article 248 grants Parliament exclusive power to make laws with respect to any matter not enumerated in the Concurrent List or the State List — i.e., residuary powers belong to Parliament.

questions.question: The Prime Minister of India is appointed by:
questions.explanation: The President appoints the Prime Minister — conventionally the leader of the majority party or coalition in the Lok Sabha.

questions.question: Under the Indian Constitution, 'Residuary Powers' lie with:
questions.explanation: Residuary powers (powers over subjects not mentioned in any of the three lists) vest in Parliament (the Union/Centre) under Article 248.

questions.question: Which constitutional provision disqualifies a person from being a member of Parliament on grounds of holding an office of profit?
questions.explanation: Article 102(1)(a) disqualifies a person from being or remaining a member of either House of Parliament if they hold any 'office of profit' under the Government of India or any State government.

questions.question: The First-Past-the-Post (FPTP) electoral system used in India means:
options: Top 2 candidates compete in a runoff
The candidate with the most votes wins, regardless of whether they have an absolute majority
Votes are proportionally distributed to parties
questions.explanation: FPTP (or plurality/simple majority system): the candidate who gets more votes than any other in the constituency wins, even without an absolute majority. India uses FPTP for Lok Sabha and State Assembly elections. Rajya Sabha and State Council elections use proportional representation.

questions.question: The Vice President of India serves as:
questions.explanation: The Vice President is the ex-officio Chairman of the Rajya Sabha (Article 64). The VP also discharges the functions of the President when the President is unable to do so.

questions.question: The President's power to promulgate Ordinances is under:
questions.explanation: Article 123 empowers the President to promulgate Ordinances when Parliament is not in session. Ordinances have the same force as Acts but must be laid before Parliament within 6 weeks of reassembly.

questions.question: The Pocket Veto (keeping a bill indefinitely) can be exercised by:
options: President when no time limit is specified for bill sent for assent
questions.explanation: For ordinary bills (not Money Bills or Constitutional Amendments), there is no time limit for Presidential assent — the President can keep it indefinitely (pocket veto), as was done by Zail Singh in 1986.

questions.question: Collective responsibility of Council of Ministers means:
options: All ministers are jointly responsible to Lok Sabha
Cabinet is responsible to Rajya Sabha
questions.explanation: Article 75: Council of Ministers is collectively responsible to Lok Sabha — all ministers stand or fall together. A vote of no-confidence against any minister means the entire government falls.

questions.question: A person who is not a member of Parliament can be appointed Minister for maximum:
questions.explanation: A non-member of Parliament can be appointed as Minister but must become a member of either House within 6 months, failing which they cease to be Minister.

questions.question: A Supreme Court judge can be removed by:
options: Address by both Houses of Parliament followed by Presidential order
questions.explanation: SC judge can be removed only on the grounds of proven misbehavior or incapacity by an address passed by special majority in both Houses of Parliament and Presidential order (Article 124).

questions.question: President's Rule (Article 356) in a state lasts initially for:
questions.explanation: President's Rule (State Emergency) lasts initially for 6 months, can be extended for a maximum of 3 years (with Parliamentary approval every 6 months after first 6 months).

questions.question: The minimum age to become Governor is:
questions.explanation: To become a Governor (Article 157): Must be a citizen of India and must have completed 35 years of age. Must not be a member of Parliament or State Legislature.

questions.question: When can the Governor reserve a bill for the President's consideration?
options: When the bill endangers High Court's position or is against Constitution
questions.explanation: Governor can reserve a bill for Presidential consideration if it: endangers the position of the High Court, is repugnant to the Constitution, or against the policy of the Union government.

questions.question: The 7th Schedule divides subjects into:
questions.explanation: 7th Schedule: (1) Union List (100 subjects) — Parliament only, (2) State List (61 subjects) — State Legislature, (3) Concurrent List (52 subjects) — Both; Parliament prevails in case of conflict.

questions.question: Which subject is in the Concurrent List?
questions.explanation: Education (Entry 25 of Concurrent List) was moved from State List to Concurrent List by the 42nd Amendment (1976). Both Parliament and State Legislatures can legislate on it.

questions.question: Residuary powers (unlisted subjects) vest with:
questions.explanation: Article 248: Residuary powers (subjects not listed in any List) vest with Parliament. This is different from USA where residuary powers vest with states.

questions.question: The Basic Structure Doctrine was established in:
questions.explanation: Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala (1973): 13-judge bench held that Parliament can amend any part of the Constitution EXCEPT its 'Basic Structure'. This doctrine is a cornerstone of Indian constitutional law.

questions.question: The Electronic Voting Machine (EVM) was first used in India in:
questions.explanation: EVMs were first used experimentally in Kerala state assembly elections in 1982 (70 constituencies). Nationwide EVM use in Lok Sabha began in 2004.

questions.question: The first Speaker of Lok Sabha was:
questions.explanation: Ganesh Vasudev Mavalankar (G.V. Mavalankar) was the first Speaker of Lok Sabha (1952-1956). He is called the 'Father of the Lok Sabha'.

questions.question: The maximum number of members in Rajya Sabha is:
questions.explanation: Rajya Sabha: maximum 250 members — 238 elected (representatives of states and UTs) + 12 nominated by President for expertise in literature, science, art, social service.

questions.question: A money bill is defined in:
questions.explanation: Article 110 defines a Money Bill — a bill that deals only with matters specified in Article 110(1): taxes, borrowing by government, appropriation from Consolidated Fund, etc.

questions.question: The Consolidated Fund of India is governed by:
questions.explanation: Article 266: Consolidated Fund of India — all revenues, loans raised, and repayments received by the Government of India go into this fund. No money can be withdrawn except through Parliamentary appropriation.

questions.question: Contingency Fund of India is established under:
questions.explanation: Article 267: Contingency Fund of India enables the President to make advances to meet unforeseen expenditure (limit ₹500 crore). Parliament approval is later sought to recoup the amount.

questions.question: Public Accounts Committee (PAC) examines:
options: Audit reports of CAG on expenditure by government
questions.explanation: PAC examines the CAG's audit reports to ensure public money was spent as Parliament intended. The chairman of PAC is traditionally from the Opposition party.

questions.question: The Estimates Committee reviews:
questions.explanation: Estimates Committee (30 members, from Lok Sabha only) examines the Estimates submitted to Parliament to suggest economies, improvements in organization, efficiency, and administrative reform.

questions.question: Which State has the highest number of seats in Lok Sabha?
questions.explanation: Uttar Pradesh has the highest number of Lok Sabha seats — 80 seats out of 543. Maharashtra (48) is second, West Bengal (42) third, Bihar (40) fourth.

questions.question: Tamil Nadu has how many seats in Lok Sabha?
questions.explanation: Tamil Nadu has 39 Lok Sabha seats — one of the highest in India. It also has 18 Rajya Sabha seats (one of the highest proportional representations).

questions.question: 'Rajya Sabha' literally means:
questions.explanation: 'Rajya Sabha' means 'Council of States' in Hindi. 'Lok Sabha' means 'House of the People'. Rajya Sabha represents the states/UTs of India in Parliament.

questions.question: A Starred Question in Parliament is:
options: An oral answer question with supplementaries
questions.explanation: A Starred Question (marked with *) requires an oral answer in the House and allows supplementary questions by the member. About 20 starred questions are listed per sitting day.

questions.question: India's first Lokpal was appointed in:
questions.explanation: Justice Pinaki Chandra Ghose became India's first Lokpal in March 2019, more than 48 years after the first Lokpal Bill was introduced in 1968.

questions.question: The Oaths of Members of Parliament are administered by:
options: President or person appointed by President
questions.explanation: Members of Parliament take oath/affirmation before the President (or a person appointed by the President) before taking their seat in Parliament.

questions.question: A 'Private Member's Bill' is a bill introduced by:
options: Any member who is not a minister
questions.explanation: A Private Member's Bill is introduced by any member of Parliament who is NOT a minister. It gets limited time (Friday afternoons). Very few private members' bills have been passed.

questions.question: The term 'Sunset Clause' in legislation means:
options: The law automatically expires after a fixed period
questions.explanation: Sunset Clause in a law means it will automatically expire (cease to be in force) after a specified date or event, unless renewed by Parliament.

questions.question: President's Rule (Art 356) requires Parliamentary approval within:
questions.explanation: President's Rule under Art 356 must be approved by both Houses of Parliament within 2 months of its proclamation. After approval, it continues for 6 months and can be extended up to 3 years.

questions.question: Under Article 3, a new State can be formed by:
questions.explanation: Article 3 empowers Parliament to form new States and alter borders by a simple majority (ordinary legislation). The President must refer the Bill to the concerned State Legislature for its views (non-binding).

questions.question: Residuary powers in India are vested with:
options: Supreme Court decides case by case
questions.explanation: Article 248 vests residuary powers (subjects not listed in any of the three Lists of Schedule 7) exclusively with Parliament. This is unlike USA where residuary powers belong to States.

questions.question: The Comptroller and Auditor General (CAG) of India submits audit reports to:
questions.explanation: The CAG submits audit reports (on accounts of the Union) to the President, who lays them before Parliament (Art 151). For State accounts, reports are submitted to the State Governor.

questions.question: Tamil Nadu has how many seats in the Lok Sabha?
questions.explanation: Tamil Nadu has 39 Lok Sabha constituencies. It had one of the earliest fully contested Lok Sabha elections and all 39 constituencies are determined by the Delimitation Commission.

questions.question: The Anti-Defection Law is contained in which Schedule?
questions.explanation: The Anti-Defection Law is in the 10th Schedule, added by the 52nd Constitutional Amendment (1985). It disqualifies members of Parliament and State Legislatures for defecting from their party.

questions.question: Disqualification under Anti-Defection Law is decided by:
questions.explanation: The Speaker of Lok Sabha/State Assembly or Chairman of Rajya Sabha/State Council decides disqualification under the 10th Schedule. This decision is subject to judicial review (Kihoto Hollohan case, 1992).

questions.question: The 'Basic Structure Doctrine' of the Indian Constitution was propounded in:
options: Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala (1973)
Minerva Mills v. Union of India (1980)
Sankari Prasad v. Union of India (1951)
questions.explanation: The Basic Structure Doctrine was laid down by a 13-judge bench in Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala (1973). It held Parliament cannot amend the 'basic structure' of the Constitution.

questions.question: The Consolidated Fund of India is dealt with in:
questions.explanation: Article 266 establishes the Consolidated Fund of India (and similar funds for States). All revenues received by Government and loans raised form part of this fund. All expenditure must be authorized by Parliament.

questions.question: The 'Joint Sitting' of both Houses of Parliament is presided over by:
questions.explanation: A joint sitting of Parliament (under Article 108) is presided over by the Speaker of Lok Sabha.

questions.question: Under the Indian Constitution, who has the power to dissolve the Lok Sabha?
options: President on advice of Prime Minister
questions.explanation: The President dissolves the Lok Sabha on the advice of the Prime Minister (after the Council of Ministers loses its majority) or on expiry of its 5-year term.

questions.question: The Parliament of India is located in:
questions.explanation: The Parliament House of India is located in New Delhi, the capital of India.

questions.question: The 'No Confidence Motion' must be passed by:
questions.explanation: A No Confidence Motion (Vote of No Confidence) against the government must be passed by a simple majority of Lok Sabha members present and voting. If passed, the PM must resign.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a part of the Indian Parliament?
questions.explanation: The Indian Parliament consists of the President, Lok Sabha, and Rajya Sabha. The Supreme Court is a separate organ of the state.

questions.question: What is the maximum strength of the Lok Sabha as provided by the Constitution of India?
questions.explanation: Article 81 caps the maximum strength of Lok Sabha at 552 members (530 from states, 20 from UTs, and 2 nominated Anglo-Indians).

questions.question: Who presides over the joint sitting of both Houses of Parliament?
questions.explanation: The Speaker of Lok Sabha presides over a joint sitting of Parliament, as per Article 108.

questions.question: A Money Bill can be introduced only in which House of Parliament?
questions.explanation: As per Article 110, a Money Bill can only be introduced in the Lok Sabha on the recommendation of the President.

questions.question: What is the minimum age required to become a member of the Rajya Sabha?
questions.explanation: Article 84 sets the minimum age for Rajya Sabha membership at 30 years, while for Lok Sabha it is 25 years.

questions.question: Which of the following is a standing committee of the Indian Parliament?
options: Committee on the Welfare of SCs and STs
questions.explanation: All three listed committees (Public Accounts, Estimates, and Welfare of SCs/STs) are standing committees of Parliament.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a power of the Parliament of India?
questions.explanation: The Chief Election Commissioner is appointed by the President, not by Parliament. Parliament's powers include constitutional amendments, impeachment of President, and budget approval.

questions.question: Which of the following motions can be moved only in the Lok Sabha?
questions.explanation: A No-Confidence Motion can be introduced only in the Lok Sabha as it tests the confidence of the Council of Ministers.

questions.question: The term 'Parliament' includes:
options: Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha only
Lok Sabha, Rajya Sabha and the President
Lok Sabha, Rajya Sabha and the Prime Minister
Lok Sabha, Rajya Sabha and the Supreme Court
questions.explanation: Article 79 states that the Parliament of India consists of the President and two Houses – Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha.

questions.question: A bill pending in the Lok Sabha lapses when:
options: The President summons a joint session
questions.explanation: A bill pending in the Lok Sabha lapses on the dissolution of the Lok Sabha. Prorogation or adjournment does not cause lapse.

questions.question: The power to decide whether a bill is a Money Bill or not rests with:
questions.explanation: As per Article 110, the Speaker of Lok Sabha decides whether a bill is a Money Bill, and his decision is final.

questions.question: Which of the following committees of Parliament has the maximum membership?
questions.explanation: The Estimates Committee has 30 members (all from Lok Sabha) and is the largest parliamentary committee.

questions.question: A private member's bill can be introduced in:
questions.explanation: A private member's bill can be introduced in either Lok Sabha or Rajya Sabha, subject to the rules.

questions.question: The 'Zero Hour' in parliamentary proceedings refers to:
options: The time immediately after Question Hour
The time when the House is adjourned
The time for voting on bills
questions.explanation: Zero Hour is the period after Question Hour when members raise matters of urgent public importance without prior notice.

questions.question: Under the Indian Constitution, the Parliament can legislate on a subject in the State List if:
options: The Rajya Sabha passes a resolution by 2/3 majority
The Supreme Court gives a directive
questions.explanation: As per Article 249, Parliament can make laws on state list if Rajya Sabha passes a resolution with 2/3 majority that it is necessary in national interest.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about the Rajya Sabha is correct?
options: It is a permanent house and not subject to dissolution
It is dissolved every five years
Its members are directly elected by the people
The Vice President is the ex-officio Chairman of the Rajya Sabha
questions.explanation: Rajya Sabha is a permanent house; one-third of its members retire every two years. The Vice President is its ex-officio Chairman.

questions.question: The minimum age for becoming a member of the Lok Sabha is
questions.explanation: Article 84(b) of the Constitution prescribes 25 years as the minimum age for Lok Sabha membership.

questions.question: Who among the following presides over a joint sitting of both Houses of Parliament?
questions.explanation: Under Article 108, the Speaker of Lok Sabha presides over a joint sitting. In their absence, the Deputy Speaker or Chairman of Rajya Sabha presides.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a device of parliamentary proceedings in India?
questions.explanation: Censure Motion is a substantive motion, not a device of proceedings. Zero Hour, Question Hour, and Point of Order are recognized devices.

questions.question: The maximum strength of the Lok Sabha as provided by the Constitution is
questions.explanation: Article 81 caps the Lok Sabha's maximum strength at 552 (530 from states, 20 from UTs, and 2 nominated Anglo-Indians).

questions.question: A money bill can be introduced only in
questions.explanation: As per Article 109, a Money Bill can only be introduced in the Lok Sabha, not in the Rajya Sabha.

questions.question: Which parliamentary committee examines the annual reports of the Comptroller and Auditor General of India?
questions.explanation: The Public Accounts Committee scrutinizes the audit reports of the CAG and ensures accountability of government expenditure.

questions.question: The tenure of a nominated member of the Rajya Sabha is
options: As long as the President desires
Co-terminus with the term of the nominating authority
questions.explanation: Nominated members of Rajya Sabha serve a six-year term, same as elected members, under Article 80.

questions.question: An ordinance promulgated by the President must be approved by Parliament within
options: 6 weeks from the reassembly of Parliament
2 months from the date of promulgation
6 months from the date of promulgation
One year from the reassembly of Parliament
questions.explanation: Under Article 123, an ordinance lapses six weeks after Parliament reassembles unless both Houses approve it earlier.

questions.question: Which of the following is a function of the Speaker of Lok Sabha?
options: Deciding the agenda of the Rajya Sabha
Conducting elections to the President's office
questions.explanation: The Speaker certifies a Money Bill under Article 110. Other functions are not vested with the Speaker.

questions.question: The maximum strength of the Lok Sabha as per the Constitution of India is?
questions.explanation: Article 81 provides for a maximum of 552 members: 530 from states, 20 from Union Territories, and 2 nominated Anglo-Indians.

questions.question: Which article of the Constitution empowers the Parliament to amend the Constitution?
questions.explanation: Article 368 lays down the procedure for amendment of the Constitution by Parliament.

questions.question: Who determines whether a Bill is a Money Bill?
questions.explanation: Under Article 110, the Speaker of Lok Sabha certifies a Money Bill and his decision is final.

questions.question: The maximum gap between two sessions of Parliament cannot exceed?
questions.explanation: Article 85 mandates that Parliament must meet at least twice a year with not more than six months between sessions.

questions.question: Which of the following is correct about the Rajya Sabha?
questions.explanation: Rajya Sabha is a permanent House not subject to dissolution; one-third of its members retire every two years.

questions.question: The proposal for removal of the Vice-President may be initiated in?
questions.explanation: Under Article 67(b), a resolution for removal of the Vice-President can be moved only in Rajya Sabha.

questions.question: The power to legislate on subjects not mentioned in any of the three lists rests with?
questions.explanation: Article 248 gives Parliament exclusive residuary power to legislate on matters not enumerated in the Union, State, or Concurrent Lists.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a parliamentary privilege?
options: Right to vote in state elections
questions.explanation: Parliamentary privileges include freedom of speech, immunity from civil arrest, and exclusive publication rights, but not a right to vote in state elections.

questions.question: As per the Constitution, the number of seats allocated to Tamil Nadu in the Lok Sabha is?
questions.explanation: Tamil Nadu has 39 Lok Sabha seats as per the Delimitation order, unchanged since 1977.

questions.question: The maximum gap between two sessions of Parliament, as per the Constitution, cannot exceed:
questions.explanation: Article 85 mandates that the gap between two sessions of Parliament shall not exceed six months.

questions.question: The 'Zero Hour' in the Lok Sabha refers to:
options: A. The time after Question Hour
B. The time before Question Hour
C. The time for private members' bills
D. The time for adjournment motion
questions.explanation: Zero Hour starts immediately after Question Hour and is not mentioned in the Rules of Procedure.

questions.question: The power to decide whether a Bill is a Money Bill rests with the:
questions.explanation: Under Article 110, the Speaker of Lok Sabha certifies a Bill as a Money Bill, and his decision is final.

questions.question: In the context of Indian Parliament, 'Vote on Account' is used to:
options: B. Withdraw money from the Consolidated Fund before the budget is passed
C. Reject a demand for grant
questions.explanation: A Vote on Account allows the government to withdraw funds for a limited period until the full budget is passed.

questions.question: The maximum strength of the Rajya Sabha as prescribed by the Constitution is:
questions.explanation: Article 80 sets the maximum strength of Rajya Sabha at 250 members (238 elected + 12 nominated).

questions.question: Which of the following is a device of Parliamentary control over the Executive?
questions.explanation: A Censure Motion is moved by the opposition to criticize the government, reflecting parliamentary control.

questions.question: The term 'Parliamentary Privileges' in India refers to:
options: A. Rights of citizens to petition Parliament
B. Special rights and immunities of MPs and committees
C. Powers of the President over Parliament
D. Rights of the media to cover debates
questions.explanation: Parliamentary privileges are special rights enjoyed by MPs and parliamentary committees to ensure their independence.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a feature of the Indian Parliament as per the Constitution?
questions.explanation: The Indian Parliament is bicameral, consisting of Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha, not unicameral.

questions.question: Under which Article of the Constitution can the Parliament legislate on a state subject if the Rajya Sabha passes a resolution by a special majority?
questions.explanation: Article 249 empowers Parliament to legislate on state subjects if Rajya Sabha passes a resolution by a two-thirds majority in national interest.

questions.question: The maximum gap between two sessions of Parliament, as per the Constitution, cannot exceed how many months?
questions.explanation: Article 85 mandates that the gap between two sessions of Parliament cannot exceed six months.

questions.question: Which of the following committees of Parliament examines the annual estimates of expenditure?
questions.explanation: The Estimates Committee examines the budget estimates and suggests economies in public expenditure.

questions.question: The 'Zero Hour' in the Indian Parliament refers to:
options: The first hour after Question Hour
The time when the House adjourns
The period for private members' bills
questions.explanation: Zero Hour starts immediately after Question Hour and allows members to raise urgent matters without prior notice.

questions.question: Who presides over a joint sitting of both Houses of Parliament?
questions.explanation: The Speaker of Lok Sabha presides over a joint sitting as per Article 108, unless the Speaker is absent.

questions.question: A resolution for the removal of the Vice President of India can be moved only in which House?
questions.explanation: As per Article 67, a resolution for removal of the Vice President can only be introduced in the Rajya Sabha.

questions.question: Which of the following is a 'State subject' under the Seventh Schedule of the Constitution, over which the Tamil Nadu Legislative Assembly has exclusive power to legislate?
questions.explanation: Public health and sanitation is a State subject under List II, while banking and atomic energy are Union subjects.

questions.question: What is the maximum gap permitted between two sessions of Parliament as per the Constitution of India?
questions.explanation: Article 85 mandates that the gap between the last sitting of one session and the first sitting of the next session shall not exceed six months.

questions.question: Who presides over a joint sitting of the two Houses of Parliament?
questions.explanation: As per Article 118(4), the Speaker of Lok Sabha presides over a joint sitting; in his absence, the Deputy Speaker presides.

questions.question: How many members can the President of India nominate to the Rajya Sabha?
questions.explanation: Article 80(3) allows the President to nominate 12 members to the Rajya Sabha having special knowledge or practical experience in literature, science, art, and social service.

questions.question: Which House of Parliament is not subject to dissolution?
questions.explanation: The Rajya Sabha is a permanent House and is not dissolved; one-third of its members retire every two years.

questions.question: Under the Anti-Defection Law, who is the final authority to decide on the disqualification of a Member of Parliament?
options: Speaker or Chairman of the House
questions.explanation: As per the Tenth Schedule, the Presiding Officer (Speaker for Lok Sabha, Chairman for Rajya Sabha) decides disqualification, subject to judicial review.

questions.question: What is the maximum strength of the Lok Sabha as per the original provisions of the Constitution?
questions.explanation: Article 81 originally provided a maximum of 552 members: 530 from states, 20 from UTs, and 2 nominated Anglo-Indians (later removed by the 104th Amendment).

questions.question: Which Parliamentary Committee examines the audited accounts and appropriation reports of the government?
questions.explanation: The Public Accounts Committee (PAC) scrutinizes the CAG reports and ensures that public funds are used as authorized by Parliament.

questions.question: What is the term of office of a member of the Rajya Sabha?
questions.explanation: A Rajya Sabha member serves a term of six years, with one-third of members retiring every two years.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a power of the Rajya Sabha?
options: Power to originate a money bill
Power to remove the Vice President
Power to authorise Parliament to legislate on a state subject
questions.explanation: Only the Lok Sabha can originate a money bill; Rajya Sabha can only recommend amendments.

questions.question: What is the maximum gap permissible between two sessions of the Indian Parliament?
questions.explanation: Article 85 stipulates that the gap between two parliamentary sessions cannot exceed six months.

questions.question: The 'Point of Order' in Parliamentary procedure relates to:
options: A matter of urgent public importance
A breach of privilege of a member
A question on the conduct of the Chair
A breach of the rules or procedure of the House
questions.explanation: A Point of Order is raised when a member believes the rules or procedure are being violated.

questions.question: Who among the following presides over the joint sitting of both Houses of Parliament?
questions.explanation: The Speaker of the Lok Sabha presides over a joint sitting, as per Article 108.

questions.question: Which constitutional amendment reduced the voting age from 21 to 18 years for Lok Sabha and state assembly elections?
questions.explanation: The 61st Amendment Act of 1989 lowered the voting age to 18 years.

questions.question: The 'Zero Hour' in the Lok Sabha begins immediately after:
questions.explanation: Zero Hour starts after the Question Hour and is an informal device to raise matters without notice.

questions.question: Under which Article of the Constitution can the Speaker of Lok Sabha be removed from office?
questions.explanation: Article 94 provides that the Speaker may be removed by a resolution of the Lok Sabha passed by an effective majority.

questions.question: A Bill which is pending in Rajya Sabha but has not been passed by Lok Sabha lapses on the dissolution of which House?
questions.explanation: A bill pending in Rajya Sabha but not passed by Lok Sabha does not lapse; only bills pending in Lok Sabha lapse.

questions.question: The maximum strength of the Lok Sabha as envisaged in the Constitution is:
questions.explanation: Article 81 caps the maximum strength of Lok Sabha at 552 (530 states + 20 UTs + 2 nominated).

questions.question: Which of the following committees of Parliament has no Rajya Sabha member?
questions.explanation: The Estimates Committee consists entirely of Lok Sabha members; Rajya Sabha has no representation.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a power of the Rajya Sabha under the Indian Constitution?
options: Power to introduce a money bill
Power to create new All India Services
questions.explanation: Money bills can only be introduced in the Lok Sabha, not the Rajya Sabha.

questions.question: The maximum gap between two sessions of Parliament in India cannot exceed which period?
questions.explanation: Article 85 mandates that Parliament must meet at least twice a year, with no more than a six-month gap between sessions.

questions.question: What is the maximum strength of the Lok Sabha as prescribed by the Constitution of India?
questions.explanation: Article 81 allows for a maximum of 552 members, including 530 from states, 20 from Union Territories, and 2 nominated Anglo-Indians.

questions.question: A bill is referred to a joint sitting of both Houses of Parliament in case of disagreement. Who presides over such a sitting?
questions.explanation: The Speaker of the Lok Sabha presides over joint sittings of Parliament.

questions.question: The principle of collective responsibility of the Council of Ministers to the Lok Sabha is enshrined in which Article of the Indian Constitution?
questions.explanation: Article 75(3) states that the Council of Ministers is collectively responsible to the Lok Sabha.

questions.question: In the context of Tamil Nadu, the number of seats allocated in the Lok Sabha is based on which Census?
questions.explanation: As per the 42nd Amendment, Lok Sabha seats are frozen based on the 1971 Census until 2026.

questions.question: Which of the following committees of Parliament is specifically concerned with examining the reports of the Comptroller and Auditor General (CAG)?
questions.explanation: The Public Accounts Committee examines CAG reports to ensure financial accountability of the government.

questions.question: A member of Parliament can be disqualified on grounds of defection under which Schedule of the Constitution?
questions.explanation: The Tenth Schedule (Anti-Defection Law) lays down grounds for disqualification of MPs and MLAs.

questions.question: Point of Order in parliamentary procedure is raised to enforce which of the following?
questions.explanation: A Point of Order is raised to draw the Speaker's attention to a breach of the rules of procedure of the House.

questions.question: The Constitution of India provides for reservation of seats for Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes in the Lok Sabha. This provision is currently valid until which year?
questions.explanation: The reservation of seats for SC/ST in the Lok Sabha was extended to 70 years from the commencement of the Constitution, i.e., until 2040.

questions.question: Which Article of the Constitution provides for the composition of the Rajya Sabha?
questions.explanation: Article 80 specifies the maximum strength and composition of the Council of States (Rajya Sabha).

questions.question: What is the maximum strength of the Lok Sabha as per the Constitution of India?
questions.explanation: As per Article 81, the maximum strength is 552 (530 from states, 20 from UTs, and 2 nominated Anglo-Indians, though the nomination provision has been removed by the 104th Amendment).

questions.question: What is the minimum number of sessions of Parliament required in a year?
questions.explanation: Article 85 mandates that there must be at least two sessions in a year, with not more than six months gap between sessions.

questions.question: When does the 'Zero Hour' in Parliament begin?
options: At the start of the day
questions.explanation: Zero Hour starts immediately after Question Hour and allows members to raise matters of urgent public importance without prior notice.

questions.question: Which type of bill can be introduced only in the Lok Sabha?
questions.explanation: A Money Bill (Article 110) can be introduced only in the Lok Sabha, and the Rajya Sabha cannot amend or reject it.

questions.question: What is the maximum permissible gap between two sessions of Parliament?
questions.explanation: The Constitution (Article 85) stipulates that not more than six months shall elapse between the last sitting of one session and the first sitting of the next.

questions.question: Who makes the final decision on whether a bill is a Money Bill?
questions.explanation: The Speaker of the Lok Sabha certifies a bill as a Money Bill, and his or her decision is final and binding.

questions.question: What is the term of a member of the Rajya Sabha?
questions.explanation: Rajya Sabha members serve a six-year term, with one-third of the members retiring every two years.

questions.question: Who is responsible for maintaining discipline and order during Question Hour in the Lok Sabha?
questions.explanation: The Speaker of the Lok Sabha ensures discipline and order during Question Hour and all proceedings of the House.

questions.question: Under Article 85 of the Indian Constitution, what is the maximum permissible gap between two sessions of Parliament?
questions.explanation: Article 85 requires Parliament to meet at least twice a year, with not more than six months elapsing between its last sitting in one session and its first sitting in the next.

questions.question: A joint sitting of both Houses of Parliament may be summoned by the President to resolve a disagreement on which type of bill?
questions.explanation: Under Article 108, a joint sitting can be convened only for an ordinary bill, as Money Bills and Constitutional Amendment Bills have separate procedures.

questions.question:
Consider the following statements regarding the introduction and passage of a Money Bill in the Indian Parliament:
1. It can be introduced only in the Lok Sabha.
2. It can be introduced only on the recommendation of the President.
3. The Rajya Sabha must return the Bill within 14 days, and its recommendations are not binding.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanation: All three statements are correct as per Article 110 and 111; Money Bill requires President's recommendation, originates in Lok Sabha, and Rajya Sabha can only recommend amendments within 14 days.

questions.question: Which of the following Parliamentary Committees is unique in that its Chairman is traditionally from the party in opposition?
questions.explanation: The Public Accounts Committee has a Chairman from the opposition party to ensure scrutiny of government expenditure; this is a long-standing convention.

questions.question: In the context of a no-confidence motion in the Lok Sabha, which of the following statements is correct?
options: It can be moved against a single minister.
It requires 60 members to support the motion.
The motion must specify a reason.
It requires support of at least 50 members to be admitted.
questions.explanation: A no-confidence motion requires the support of at least 50 members to be admitted in the Lok Sabha; it is against the entire council of ministers, no reason needed.

questions.question:
Consider the following statements about the joint sitting of Parliament:
1. It is convened by the Speaker of the Lok Sabha.
2. A Money Bill cannot be referred to a joint sitting.
3. The joint sitting is presided over by the Speaker of the Lok Sabha.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Joint sitting is convened by the President (not Speaker), so 1 is wrong; Money Bill is exempted (2 correct); Speaker presides (3 correct).

questions.question: A resolution for the removal of the Vice-President of India can be moved only in which House of Parliament?
questions.explanation: As per Article 67(b), a resolution for removal of the Vice-President can be moved only in the Rajya Sabha, where he is the ex-officio Chairman.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a function of the Speaker of the Lok Sabha?
options: Deciding whether a Bill is a Money Bill
Disqualifying members on grounds of defection
Casting vote in case of a tie
Nominating members to the Rajya Sabha
questions.explanation: The Speaker does not nominate Rajya Sabha members; that power lies with the President. Other three are Speaker's functions.

questions.question:
Consider the following statements regarding an ordinance promulgated by the President:
1. It must be laid before both Houses of Parliament when they reassemble.
2. It ceases to operate if not approved by both Houses within six weeks of reassembly.
3. The President can withdraw an ordinance at any time.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanation: All three are correct under Article 123; ordinance must be laid before Parliament, ceases after 6 weeks if not approved, and can be withdrawn by President.

questions.question: The provisions regarding the representation of Union Territories in the Lok Sabha are mentioned in which Article of the Constitution?
questions.explanation: Article 81 deals with composition of Lok Sabha, including representation from states and Union Territories; Article 80 covers Rajya Sabha.

questions.question: A Constitutional Amendment Bill passed by both Houses of Parliament requires ratification by at least half of the state legislatures. Which of the following amendments does NOT require such ratification?
questions.explanation: Amendments affecting Fundamental Rights are not among the matters requiring state ratification under Article 368(2). The others relate to federal structure.

questions.question: The term 'lame duck session' in the context of Indian Parliament refers to:
options: The session after the budget presentation
The session after the election results are declared but before the new government takes oath
The session immediately following the President's address
The session when a Money Bill is passed without debate
questions.explanation: A lame duck session occurs when Parliament meets after the election results but before the newly elected Lok Sabha is formed; it is a US term adapted to Indian context.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about the 'Office of Profit' under Article 102(1)(a) is correct, as per the Parliament (Prevention of Disqualification) Act, 1959, and subsequent judicial interpretations?
options: A. It includes any position that receives a salary from the government, regardless of the nature of work.
B. It excludes positions held by ministers and parliamentary secretaries in all states.
C. It does not include offices held by members of Parliament as part of a parliamentary delegation abroad.
D. It includes the office of a government contractor who supplies goods to the state.
questions.explanation: The Act exempts certain offices from disqualification, and courts have held that ad-hoc delegations or positions without pecuniary gain beyond reimbursement are not 'offices of profit'.

questions.question: Consider the following statements regarding the 'Money Bill' under Article 110: (1) A bill is a Money Bill if it contains only provisions related to taxes, borrowing, or the Consolidated Fund. (2) The Speaker's decision on whether a bill is a Money Bill is final and cannot be challenged in any court. Which of the above statements is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Statement 1 is incorrect because a Money Bill can also include incidental matters; statement 2 is correct as per Article 110(3), though subject to limited judicial review in exceptional cases.

questions.question: Under the Anti-Defection Law (Tenth Schedule), which of the following scenarios does NOT attract disqualification from Parliament?
options: A. A member voluntarily gives up membership of their political party.
B. A member votes against their party's whip in a confidence motion.
C. A member merges with another party when two-thirds of the party's legislators agree.
D. A member abstains from voting on a financial bill despite a party whip.
questions.explanation: Merger is valid if at least two-thirds of the party's legislators agree, as per paragraph 4 of the Tenth Schedule; other options trigger disqualification.

questions.question: Which of the following is a correct distinction between the 'Lok Sabha' and the 'Rajya Sabha' regarding their financial powers?
options: A. Rajya Sabha can reject a Money Bill, but Lok Sabha can override it with a joint sitting.
B. Rajya Sabha can only recommend amendments to a Money Bill, which Lok Sabha may accept or reject.
C. Both Houses have equal power to introduce a Money Bill.
D. Rajya Sabha has the power to veto the annual budget.
questions.explanation: Under Article 109, Rajya Sabha must return a Money Bill within 14 days with recommendations, but Lok Sabha is not bound to accept them.

questions.question: In the context of parliamentary privileges, which of the following is NOT a privilege enjoyed by individual members of Parliament in India?
options: A. Freedom from arrest in civil cases during the session and 40 days before and after.
B. Freedom of speech in Parliament without being subject to any court proceedings.
C. Right to exclude strangers from the House during debates.
questions.explanation: The right to exclude strangers is a collective privilege of the House, not an individual privilege of a member.

questions.question: Consider the following: (1) The President can nominate two members to the Lok Sabha from the Anglo-Indian community. (2) The President can nominate 12 members to the Rajya Sabha from fields like literature, science, and social service. Which of the above provisions has been abolished by the 104th Constitutional Amendment Act, 2019?
questions.explanation: The 104th Amendment extended the reservation for SC/STs but abolished the nomination of Anglo-Indians to Lok Sabha and state assemblies.

questions.question: A bill is pending in the Lok Sabha but not passed. The Lok Sabha is dissolved. What happens to the bill under Article 107?
options: A. It automatically becomes law after six months.
B. It lapses and must be reintroduced in the new Lok Sabha.
C. It is referred to the Rajya Sabha for final decision.
D. It is sent to the President for promulgation as an ordinance.
questions.explanation: Under Article 107(3), any bill pending in the Lok Sabha lapses on dissolution, except bills pending in the Rajya Sabha or at the President's assent stage.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes the 'Joint Sitting' of Parliament under Article 108?
options: A. It is summoned by the President to resolve a deadlock on any bill, including Money Bills.
B. It is presided over by the Speaker of the Lok Sabha, and decisions are taken by a simple majority.
C. It can be convened only after the Rajya Sabha rejects a bill twice.
D. It requires a two-thirds majority of the total membership to pass a bill.
questions.explanation: Joint sitting is presided by the Speaker, and decisions are by simple majority; it excludes Money Bills and Constitution Amendment bills.

questions.question: Under the 'Question Hour' in Parliament, which type of question requires a member to give a 15-day notice and is answered orally by the minister?
questions.explanation: A starred question requires 15 days' notice and is answered orally, allowing supplementary questions; unstarred questions get written answers.

questions.question: Which of the following is a correct statement regarding the 'Committee on Public Accounts' (PAC) in the Indian Parliament?
options: A. It examines the accounts of the government and the appropriation of funds, and its chairman is from the ruling party.
B. It examines the reports of the Comptroller and Auditor General (CAG), and its chairman is from the opposition.
C. It is a joint committee of both Houses, and its members are nominated by the President.
D. It has the power to summon the Prime Minister for questioning on financial matters.
questions.explanation: The PAC examines CAG reports on appropriation, and by convention, its chairman is from the opposition party.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about the 'Office of Profit' under Article 102(1)(a) is correct, as per recent Supreme Court interpretations?
options: It disqualifies a Member of Parliament if the office is held under the Central or State Government and carries remuneration beyond actual expenses.
It disqualifies a Member of Parliament only if the office is created by a statute and the holder is entitled to a salary.
It disqualifies a Member of Parliament if the office is held under a government company, regardless of remuneration.
It disqualifies a Member of Parliament only if the office is held under the Union Government, not under a State Government.
questions.explanation: The Supreme Court has held that the test for 'Office of Profit' is whether the office is under the government and carries remuneration beyond compensatory allowance, leading to disqualification under Article 102.
